data.text/html:
Does Attention Need Itself?

Diagnosing and Fixing the Attention Sink

Look at the bright vertical stripe on the left edge of this heatmap. Every token in the sequence is staring at the very first token — even though that token is just the word "The." That's an attention sink: a token that absorbs far more attention than it deserves.Notice anything weird about the first column?

data.text/markdown:
What is attention?

Every word in a sentence "looks at" every other word to figure out which ones are most important for understanding it. The model divides 100% of its attention budget across all tokens. More attention = "I think you're important."

What is a sink?

In transformer models, the very first token receives a disproportionate share of attention — often 40-60% in deeper layers — regardless of what word it actually is. Researchers call this an attention sink (Xiao et al., 2023; Chen et al., LeCun/NYU).

Why does it matter?

Sinks waste model capacity. Heads that should be tracking syntax or meaning are instead staring at a meaningless token. This distorts representations and, as we'll show later, hurts memory compression.

data.text/html:
Can we fix it by blocking self-attention?

One hypothesis: maybe sinks form because tokens attend to themselves too much. Exclusive Self Attention (Zhai, 2025) blocks the diagonal — each token can look at every other token, but not itself. Toggle it on and watch what happens to the sink stripe.

Finding: Exclusive Self-Attention doesn't fix the sink

The two lines below overlap almost perfectly. Blocking self-attention has no effect on sink magnitude. This tells us something important: sinks aren't caused by tokens looking at themselves. They're deeper — likely tied to the pre-norm architecture and how residual streams accumulate. This is an original empirical finding connecting Exclusive Self Attention (Zhai, curated paper #6) and attention sink research (LeCun/NYU).

data.text/markdown:
Two Fixes, Opposite Philosophies

If self-attention isn't the cause, what actually works?

Fix 1: Temperature Scaling — Cool down the math

Softmax forces attention to sum to 100%. When nothing is relevant, the model must dump excess somewhere — the sink. By dividing attention scores by a temperature T > 1 before softmax, we flatten the distribution. The model spreads attention more evenly instead of concentrating it.

Fix 2: Sink Token — Give it a designated target

Prepend a "garbage" token at position 0. If the model needs a dump target, give it one on purpose. The garbage token absorbs the junk. The real first token is freed — its attention drops from ~59% to less than 1%. One fix changes the function. The other changes the data. Both work — for different reasons.

data.text/html:
<div style='display: flex;flex: 1;flex-direction: column;justify-content: flex-start;align-items: normal;flex-wrap: nowrap;gap: 0.5rem'><marimo-mime-renderer data-mime='"application/vnd.marimo+mimebundle"' data-data='"{\"image/png\": \"data:image/png;base64,\", \"__metadata__\": {\"image/png\": {\"width\": 1290, \"height\": 488}}}"'></marimo-mime-renderer><span class="markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents"><span class="paragraph"><strong>Layer 3 — attention to first real token:</strong>
Standard: <strong>0.394</strong> |
Temperature T=3: <strong>0.143</strong> (-64%) |
Sink Token: <strong>0.0059</strong> (-98%)</span></span></div>

Attention Health Dashboard

A new way to see which parts of the model are sick (sink-corrupted) and which are healthy. Each cell in the grid below is one attention head. Color shows its entropy — how spread out its attention is.
  • Blue = healthy — attention distributed across relevant tokens
  • Red = sick — attention concentrated (likely on the sink)
Toggle between conditions and watch heads heal.

<div style='display: flex;flex: 1;flex-direction: column;justify-content: flex-start;align-items: normal;flex-wrap: nowrap;gap: 0.5rem'><marimo-mime-renderer data-mime='"application/vnd.marimo+mimebundle"' data-data='"{\"image/png\": \"data:image/png;base64,\", \"__metadata__\": {\"image/png\": {\"width\": 922, \"height\": 588}}}"'></marimo-mime-renderer><span class="markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents"><span class="paragraph"><strong>44</strong> of 144 heads remain sick (was 45 in standard). <strong>1 heads healed.</strong></span></span></div>

data.text/markdown:
What the dashboard reveals

Not all low-entropy heads are sick. Some heads are supposed to focus narrowly — syntax heads that track subject-verb agreement, or coreference heads that link pronouns to nouns. These have low entropy in every condition. The sink-corrupted heads are the ones that have low entropy only in standard attention but recover when you apply a fix. The sink was hijacking their attention budget. A healthy model has a mix of focused and diffuse heads. The sink corrupts this mix by making too many heads focused on the wrong thing.

data.text/html:
Why It Matters: KV Cache Compression

When deploying language models, you compress the key-value cache to save memory. Compression keeps the tokens with the highest attention and discards the rest. But if the sink token is hoarding attention, the compressor wastes budget keeping a useless token while discarding informative ones. Connects to curated paper #9: Fast KV Compaction via Attention Matching (Zweiger et al., 2026)At 50% compression: Standard retains 49% of content tokens. Temperature T=3 retains 49% — that's +0pp more content. Sink Token retains 50% — +0pp more content. Fixing the sink doesn't just clean up attention — it makes your model cheaper to deploy.
Built for the alphaXiv × marimo notebook competition. Primary paper: Exclusive Self Attention (Zhai, curated #6). Connected paper: Fast KV Compaction (Zweiger et al., curated #9). References: Attention Sinks (Xiao et al., 2023), The Spike, the Sparse and the Sink (Chen et al., LeCun/NYU), Attention Sinks Are Provably Necessary (2026).
